protocol_definition.name: Debate
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, critique competing answers, and Writer synthesizes.
protocol_definition.visibility: Initial answers are private; critique round exposes competing answers.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after the configured critique rounds and Writer output.

final_call.expected_action: Synthesize the strongest factually corrected answer. Resolve disagreements against the task constraints and return only the self-contained requested deliverable.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Assumptions and non-goals
- Assumptions
  - Inbound volume: ~10,000 tickets/day (~7 per second on average), with bursts.
  - Tenants: 20 enterprise tenants, each with per-tenant routing rules and audit needs.
  - Personal data: tickets may contain PII; redaction must occur before any third-party model call.
  - Desired steady-state p95 latency target: 2 seconds for automated classification and routing under normal load.
  - Reliability requirement: fail-safe behavior—no ticket loss; tickets must be processed or queued safely.
  - Observability and auditing: tenant-aware logs, with ability to reconstruct events per tenant.
- Non-goals
  - Real-time human-in-the-loop for all tickets (only high-risk/uncertain get routed to humans).
  - External vendor integration specifics; no vendor-specific costs or SLAs cited.
  - Precise hardware sizing or cloud-provider specifics; focus on architecture and behavior.

2. Component diagram described in text
- Ingestion Layer
  - API gateway / queueing layer (per-tenant routing aware)
  - Ticket intake microservice (staging area, basic validation)
- Redaction Service
  - PII detection and redaction module
  - Output fed to classifier
- Classification & Routing Layer
  - Issue type classifier
  - Urgency classifier
  - Policy engine (per-tenant routing rules)
  - Dual-path router: automated path vs. human-review path
- Redaction Gate
  - Ensures redaction happens before any third-party call
- Third-Party Model Orchestration
  - Model API adapters (for classification and potential enrichment)
  - Retry/backoff and circuit-breaker controls
- Human Review Queue
  - High-risk or uncertain tickets sent here
  - Moderation UI and workflow
- Audit and Logging Layer
  - Tenant-aware logs with immutable append-only storage
  - Audit index for compliance and debugging
- Data Security and Privacy Layer
  - Access controls, encryption at rest/in transit
  - PII handling policy enforcement
- Observability
  - Metrics, tracing, dashboards, alerting
- Storage
  - Durable storage for tickets, processed results, routing decisions, and audit trails
- Failure Handling / Safe Fall-Back
  - Dead-letter queues, backpressure signals, graceful degradation paths

3. End-to-end data flow
- Ticket arrives via Ingestion Layer (per-tenant context attached).
- Basic validation; enqueue to a durable processing queue.
- Redaction Service receives raw ticket; outputs redacted text and a redaction quality signal.
- Classification & Routing Layer consumes redacted ticket; runs issue-type classifier and urgency classifier.
- Policy Engine applies tenant-specific routing rules to determine automated routing vs. human review.
- If automated path: persist results, push to downstream systems, and write tenant-aware audit logs.
- If high-risk/uncertain: route ticket to Human Review Queue; log routing decision; await human disposition.
- All model calls are preceded by the Redaction Gate to ensure no unredacted data leaves the system.
- Outputs (classification, urgency, routing decision) are stored with tenant context and auditable events.
- Observability collects metrics and traces across services; failures emit alerts without dropping tickets.
- In case of failure (e.g., redaction or classifier issues), tickets are moved to dead-letter or retry queues with backoff, ensuring no data loss.

4. Key services and responsibilities table
- Ingestion Service
  - Responsibilities: receive tickets, basic validation, attach tenant context, enqueue for processing.
- Redaction Service
  - Responsibilities: detect PII, redact before any downstream call, provide redaction confidence.
- Classifier Service
  - Responsibilities: predict issue type and urgency; expose confidence scores; adhere to 2s p95 under normal load.
- Policy Engine
  - Responsibilities: evaluate tenant-specific routing rules; decide automated vs. human routing; support per-tenant customization.
- Router
  - Responsibilities: route tickets to automated workflow or to Human Review Queue; enforce fail-safe routing when downstream components are degraded.
- Human Review Queue and UI
  - Responsibilities: present high-risk/uncertain tickets to humans; capture disposition and feedback.
- Audit Log Service
  - Responsibilities: write tenant-aware, immutable logs for all actions; provide tamper-evident audit trails.
- Model Orchestrator
  - Responsibilities: coordinate model calls, apply redaction gate, manage retries, timeout handling, and backpressure.
- Data Security & Privacy Layer
  - Responsibilities: enforce encryption, access controls, data masking, and PII handling policies.
- Observability & Telemetry
  - Responsibilities: collect metrics, traces, logs; provide dashboards; enable alerting.
- Storage Layer
  - Responsibilities: durable storage for tickets, results, and audit trails with tenant isolation.

5. Tenant isolation and privacy safeguards
- Data separation: per-tenant namespaces for queues, storage buckets, and audit logs; strict RBAC to tenant data.
- Redaction-first policy: redaction occurs before any external model call; raw PII never leaves the secure boundary.
- Per-tenant routing rules: policy engine evaluates rules scoped to tenant; changes require proper authorization and versioning.
- Immutable audit logs: all events written to an append-only store with per-tenant indexing; tamper-evidence.
- Access controls: least-privilege principals for services; service-to-service authentication with mTLS; encryption at rest and in transit.
- PII minimization: only the necessary data fields are retained in downstream systems; redaction preserves context where needed for triage.

6. Reliability and failure-handling plan
- Guaranteed no-drop approach: use durable queues with backpressure, retry with exponential backoff, and dead-letter queues for irrecoverable cases.
- Idempotency: idempotent processing across retries to avoid duplicates.
- Graceful degradation: if classifier times out or redaction lags, tickets remain in processing with a visible “pending” state and queued retries; human review can still access context.
- Fail-open vs fail-safe routing: default to safe path (human review) when components degrade, ensuring no automated misclassification or data leakage.
- Backpressure and queueing: central queuing layer applies rate limits and burst handling; autoscale policies tied to queue depth.
- Observability-driven recovery: automatic alerts on latency degradation, elevated error rates; auto-triggered remediation playbooks.
- Audit-first guarantees: every action associated with a ticket is auditable regardless of state.

7. Latency and throughput reasoning
- Target: 2-second p95 for automated classification and routing under normal load.
- Critical path components: Ingestion -> Redaction -> Classification -> Policy Engine -> Router -> Result persistence.
- Parallelism: redaction and classification can run concurrently for multiple tickets; per-tenant sharding reduces cross-tenant interference.
- Caching: model results can be cached for repeated patterns if applicable; policy rules are lightweight lookups.
- Queuing safeguards: use fast, persistent queues; backpressure prevents overload; dead-lettering for irrecoverable items.
- Burst handling: autoscale processing workers to manage spikes; maintain p95 by distributing load and avoiding cold starts.

8. Failure-injection matrix for A, B, and C
A. Redaction service returns low confidence for a ticket containing possible PII
- Observed state transition: Ticket enters redaction with low confidence; fallback to conservative redaction (mask more aggressively) or escalate to human review for potential PII; downstream classifier receives redacted output with increased uncertainty marker.
- User/operator visibility: System surfaces redaction confidence in audit logs and a UI flag indicating potential PII; operators may opt to route to human review for confirmation.
- Retry/recovery behavior: If confidence is low, do not bypass human review; retry redaction at higher sensitivity or route to manual review; may temporarily pause automated processing for that ticket.
- Audit event: Redaction confidence score, redaction policy decision, escalation to human review if needed.
- Final safe outcome: Ticket either proceeds with conservative redaction and automated routing if acceptable, or is routed to human review for confirmation; no PII leaks occur.

B. Classifier reaches a 1.2-second timeout while queue depth is rising
- Observed state transition: Classification step times out; system marks ticket as pending; subsequent steps may hold, queue depth increases; potential cascade toward backpressure or human review.
- User/operator visibility: Operators see elevated latency and potential backlog; dashboards show p95 and queue depth; tickets flagged for retry or human review if threshold breached.
- Retry/recovery behavior: Implement exponential backoff; retry once or twice before routing to human review for urgent cases; circuit-breaker opens to prevent cascading failures.
- Audit event: Classification timeout, queue depth, retry attempt, routing decision adjustment.
- Final safe outcome: If backlog persists but no data loss, tickets can be routed to human review or temporarily paused with clear visibility; automated path degraded but safe.

C. Two administrators publish conflicting routing-rule versions for the same tenant
- Observed state transition: Policy engine receives conflicting versions; ambiguity in routing decisions; last-writer-wins or consensus mechanism determines which version is active.
- User/operator visibility: Audit logs show both versions and the resolution; UI indicates active version and potential conflicts; may trigger an explicit conflict-resolution workflow.
- Retry/recovery behavior: Conflict resolution process prompts admins to reconcile versions; systems may lock tenant policy during resolution; eventual consistency achieved via versioned rules with a clear active version.
- Audit event: Policy version conflict detected, resolution actions, active policy version after merge.
- Final safe outcome: A single, verified routing rule version remains active; conflicting versions are reconciled to avoid inconsistent routing; no tickets are misrouted.

9. Architecture decision records
- ADR 1: Redaction-before-any-model-call policy
  - Selected option: Enforce redaction gate before any third-party model interaction; all downstream inputs are redacted.
  - Rejected alternative: Redaction after model call or optional redaction per route.
  - Rationale: Prevents PII leakage, simplifies model privacy guarantees, and ensures a consistent data-sanitized input for all models.
- ADR 2: Per-tenant routing policy with versioning
  - Selected option: Each tenant has a versioned policy set; routing engine references the active version per ticket; changes require explicit promotion to active with audit trail.
  - Rejected alternative: Global or one-size-fits-all routing rules; immediate effect of changes without versioning.
  - Rationale: Supports tenant-specific customization, auditability, and safe rollout/rollback of routing changes.
- ADR 3: Fail-safe default routing to human review under degraded conditions
  - Selected option: If latency or component failure is detected beyond thresholds, route to human review by default rather than risk auto-action.
  - Rejected alternative: Continue automated processing with degraded defaults; risk higher misclassification.
  - Rationale: Prioritizes safety and data privacy, ensuring human oversight during instability.
- ADR 4: Immutable, tenant-aware audit logs
  - Selected option: All events appended to tenant-scoped, tamper-evident logs; write-once storage with strong ordering guarantees.
  - Rejected alternative: Centralized, non-tenant-scoped logs.
  - Rationale: Enables accountability, forensic analysis, and tenant isolation.
- ADR 5: Backpressure-driven autoscaling plan
  - Selected option: Tie worker pool size to queue depth with cooldowns; enable auto-scaling policies per tenant and global late-binding queues.
  - Rejected alternative: Fixed capacity; reactive scaling only after outages.
  - Rationale: Maintains p95 latency targets under load while avoiding thrashing.

10. Observability, testing, and rollout plan
- Observability
  - Metrics: p95 latency for classification, queue depth, redaction confidence, model time, failure rates, human-review counts, per-tenant routing rule hits.
  - Tracing: end-to-end traces across ingestion, redaction, classification, routing, and storage.
  - Logging: tenant-scoped audit logs with versioned policy references.
- Testing
  - Unit tests: redaction correctness, model input integrity, policy evaluation correctness.
  - Integration tests: end-to-end ticket processing under controlled load; failure-injection tests for A, B, C.
  - Chaos testing: simulate redaction low-confidence, classifier timeouts, and policy conflicts to validate fail-safe behavior.
  - Load testing: synthetic traffic to meet 2s p95 under peak simulated load; verify backpressure behavior.
- Rollout plan
  - Canary: deploy to a subset of tenants; monitor latency and correctness.
  - Gradual rollout: increment tenant coverage; enable feature flags for routing policy changes and redaction behavior.
  - Rollback capability: maintain previous stable versions; quick rollback path for policy or redaction issues.
  - Incident playbooks: predefined responses for A, B, C failure injections with escalation steps.

11. Major risks and mitigations
- PII leakage risk if redaction fails
  - Mitigation: enforce redaction gate; multi-pass redaction with confidence checks; human review for low-confidence cases.
- Latency spikes and p95 target risk
  - Mitigation: autoscale workers, per-tenant sharding, backpressure, and circuit breaks; prioritize critical tickets.
- Policy conflict and inconsistent routing
  - Mitigation: versioned policies with audit trail; automated conflict detection and admin resolution workflow.
- Data privacy and tenant isolation gaps
  - Mitigation: strict RBAC, per-tenant namespaces, encryption in transit/rest, regular security reviews.
- Human review queue bottlenecks
  - Mitigation: SLA targets for human review, tiered escalation, capacity planning, and workload distribution.

12. Final recommendation
- Implement a tenant-aware, redaction-first, multi-tenant triage architecture as described, with:
  - Redaction-before-model-Calls gating to prevent data leakage.
  - Per-tenant, versioned routing policies to enable safe, customizable routing.
  - Fail-safe default routing to human review during degraded conditions to avoid automated misclassification.
  - Durable, tenant-scoped audit logs with immutable storage.
  - Backpressure-driven reliability and autoscaling to meet 2s p95 latency under normal load and preserve ticket integrity during bursts.
- Adopt the failure-injection plan A, B, and C to validate resilience:
  - A: Ensure low-confidence redaction escalates or conservatively redacts with human review as needed.
  - B: Implement timeout handling and backpressure to avoid cascading failures; route to human review when needed.
  - C: Enforce conflict resolution workflow for policy versions; ensure a single active policy per tenant.
- Emphasize robust testing, staged rollouts, and strong observability to detect deviations early and roll back safely if needed.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Assumptions and non-goals
- Assumptions
  - 10,000 tickets/day ≈ ~7 per minute sustained, with occasional spikes; 20 enterprise tenants with distinct routing rules.
  - Classification types: issue type (e.g., Billing, Technical, Access, etc.) and urgency (e.g., Low/Med/High/Critical).
  - Personal data may appear in ticket content; redaction must occur before any third-party model call.
  - Automatic classification/routing target latency: p95 <= 2 seconds under normal load.
  - High-risk or uncertain tickets require human review handoff.
  - Tenant-specific configuration, audit logging, and isolation are required.
  - Failures should fail-safe: no ticket dropped; tickets queued and retriable; preserving data integrity.
- Non-goals
  - Real-time human-in-the-loop decision for every ticket (only for high-risk/uncertain cases).
  - End-to-end cost optimization or vendor-specific implementations.
  - Exact vendor benchmarks or unprovided performance data.

2. Component diagram described in text
- Ingress API Gateway: receives tickets, enforces authentication, rate limiting, and tenant context.
- Message Bus / Queue Layer: decouples ingestion from processing; supports per-tenant queues and backpressure signals.
- Redaction Service: performs PII redaction on ticket content prior to any external model call; emits redaction confidence and audit events.
- Classifier Service: stateless or scalable microservice that classifies issue type and urgency; uses tenant-specific routing rules; emits classification results and latency metrics.
- Routing Engine: determines whether to route to automated pipeline or human review; applies tenant-specific routing rules; supports conflicting-rule detection.
- Human Review Workbench: UI and workflow for escalations; tracks review status and ties back to tickets.
- Audit Log Service: writes immutable, tenant-scoped audit logs for all actions (ingress, redaction, classification, routing decisions, human reviews).
- Identity & Access Management: enforces tenant isolation, permissions, and admin actions.
- Persistence Layer: ticket store with versioned routing rules, per-tenant configuration, and eventual consistency guarantees.
- Observability and Telemetry: metrics, traces, logs, dashboards; anomaly detection hooks.
- Failure-Handling Layer: circuit breakers, backoff/retry policies, dead-letter queues, and safe-fail pathways.

3. End-to-end data flow
- Ticket arrives at Ingress API Gateway with tenant context.
- Tenant rules fetched from Tenant Config Service (cached with invalidation).
- Ticket is persisted to a durable ticket store; a message is published to the Ingestion Topic on the Message Bus.
- Redaction Service subscribes to the Ingestion Topic, redacts PII, publishes redacted payload to a Redacted-For-ML topic along with redaction confidence.
- Classifier Service subscribes to Redacted-For-ML topic, performs classification (issue type, urgency) using tenant-specific routing rules; returns classification result with latency.
- Routing Engine uses classification and tenant rules to determine:
  - If auto-routing to downstream automation is allowed.
  - If high-risk or uncertain, escalate to Human Review.
  - If none, proceed with automated routing to the appropriate queue (e.g., self-service KB, SLA-driven handler).
- If escalation is triggered, an item is placed in the Human Review Workbench queue for the corresponding tenant; audit events emitted.
- All actions are written to Audit Log Service with tenant-scoped identifiers and timestamps.
- Ticket state updated in the Ticket Store; metrics surfaced to Observability layer.

4. Key services and responsibilities table
- Ingress API Gateway
  - Responsibilities: intake, authZ, tenant scoping, basic validation, routing to queues.
- Tenant Config Service
  - Responsibilities: store per-tenant routing rules, contact points, and policy constraints; provide versioning.
- Redaction Service
  - Responsibilities: redact PII, return redaction confidence, post-redaction payload.
- Classifier Service
  - Responsibilities: issue type and urgency classification; produce per-tenant routing signals; enforce p95 latency target.
- Routing Engine
  - Responsibilities: apply tenant rules, ensure consistency, detect conflicting rule versions, decide automated vs human routing.
- Human Review Workbench
  - Responsibilities: present tickets requiring review, capture decisions, update ticket state.
- Audit Log Service
  - Responsibilities: immutable per-tenant logs for all actions; support eDiscovery and retrospectives.
- Persistence Layer
  - Responsibilities: durable storage of tickets, routing rules, audit events; supports versioning and rollback.
- Observability/Monitoring
  - Responsibilities: collect latency, error rates, queue depths, redaction confidences; alert on SLA breaches.
- Identity & Access Management
  - Responsibilities: tenant isolation, admin roles, RBAC enforcement.

5. Tenant isolation and privacy safeguards
- Tenant-scoped queues and data stores: all data tagged with tenant_id; no cross-tenant data access.
- Redaction prior to third-party model calls: guarantees that sensitive content does not leave the internal system unredacted.
- Per-tenant routing rule versions: changes published with explicit version numbers; routing decisions reference the active version per tenant.
- Immutable audit logs: append-only logs with tenant context; tamper-evident storage (e.g., cryptographic signing of logs).
- Access controls: admins scoped per tenant; cross-tenant visibility disabled in UI and APIs.
- Data minimization: only necessary fields persisted for processing; PII eliminated or tokenized post-redaction.

6. Reliability and failure-handling plan
- Fail-safe defaults: if any component is unhealthy, tickets continue through safe path (queued, redacted, classified with best-effort minimal data) and routed to human review if uncertainty is detected.
- Backpressure and queueing: per-tenant queues with backpressure signals; high load triggers auto-throttle, queue-based retry with exponential backoff.
- Redundancy: multiple instances of critical services; stateless design for classifier/redaction; durable persistent stores with replication.
- Dead-letter and retries: failed messages go to per-tenant Dead-Letter Queues; retry policies with escalation to human review if repeated failures.
- Observability-driven rollback: if routing rule version conflict detected (C), system prevents auto-routing until resolved; escalates to admin view with audit logs.
- Safe-fail for PII redaction: if redaction service errors, ticket copied to a human-in-the-loop path with redacted placeholders and notification.

7. Latency and throughput reasoning
- Target: p95 ≤ 2 seconds for automated classification and routing under normal load.
- Ingestion: 10k tickets/day equals ~7 per minute; peak bursts must be handled via autoscaled workers and elastic queues.
- Per-ticket latency budget: ~1 second for redaction + ~0.5 second for classification + ~0.3 second for routing decisions under normal load; plus overhead for I/O and audit logging. In practice, provide headroom for tail latency by using parallelism (redaction and classification in parallel where safe) and caching of tenant config.
- Backpressure handling: if queue depth rises, downstream services must throttle, while the Ingress Gateways accumulate tickets and push into a prioritized backlog or to human review when automatic path cannot meet latency targets.

8. Failure-injection matrix for A, B, and C
A. Redaction service returns low confidence for a ticket containing possible PII.
- Observed state transition: Ticket arrives -> redaction attempted -> low confidence raised -> system flags potential PII risk; redaction may be incomplete or degraded; fallback to escalation path.
- User/operator visibility: alerts to security/ops; audit log notes redaction confidence and fallback action.
- Retry/recovery: retry redaction with alternate model or fallback to manual redaction; if still low confidence, route to human review; do not proceed to third-party model with unredacted data.
- Audit event: redaction_confidence_low event; ticket_id, tenant_id, timestamp, retry attempts, escalation status.
- Final safe outcome: ticket is either reprocessed with improved redaction or sent to Human Review with redaction placeholders in automated systems, ensuring no unredacted PII leaves internal boundary.

B. The classifier reaches a 1.2-second timeout while queue depth is rising.
- Observed state transition: Ingress -> redaction -> classifier -> timeout; latency breach detected; queue depth increases indicating backpressure.
- User/operator visibility: SLA breach alert; potential degraded auto-routing; operators can intervene.
- Retry/recovery: backoff and retry with exponential backoff; if persistent, route to Human Review; possibly scale classifier workers.
- Audit event: classifier_timeout_latency, queue_depth_warning; timestamps and retry counts.
- Final safe outcome: system avoids dropping tickets; escalates to human review or uses cached/rule-based routing as fallback; classification results may be deferred but ticket remains in-progress.

C. Two administrators publish conflicting routing-rule versions for the same tenant.
- Observed state transition: Tenant rule fetch returns version A and B; routing engine detects conflict; auto-routing blocked for that tenant.
- User/operator visibility: Admin alert; UI shows conflict and requires resolution; audit events capture rule version mismatch.
- Retry/recovery: conflict resolution workflow initiated; admins are prompted to reconcile versions; once resolved, active version selected; possibly a lock or consensus mechanism to avoid race.
- Audit event: routing_rule_conflict_detected, tenant_id, versions_in_conflict, resolution_status.
- Final safe outcome: automated routing disabled for that tenant until admins resolve; human review path unaffected; no tickets dropped.

9. Architecture decision records
- ADR-01: Redaction-before-ML by default
  - Selected option: Enforce redaction prior to any third-party model calls; even if redaction fails, escalate to human review with placeholders.
  - Rejected alternative: Post-ML redaction or rely on model-level redaction.
  - Rationale: Prioritizes privacy and tenant trust; reduces risk of data leakage to external models; aligns with privacy safeguards.
- ADR-02: Per-tenant routing rule versioning with conflict detection
  - Selected option: Each tenant has a versioned rule set; routing engine detects conflicting versions and blocks auto-routing until resolved.
  - Rejected alternative: Last-writer-wins without conflict awareness.
  - Rationale: Avoids inconsistent routing decisions; ensures auditability and predictable behavior per tenant.
- ADR-03: Fail-safe escalation path for latency breaches
  - Selected option: If classifier latency exceeds threshold or health degraded, automatically escalate to Human Review rather than silently retrying.
  - Rejected alternative: Silent retries with backoff until success, risking SLA breach.
  - Rationale: Ensures no tickets are dropped or misrouted under high load; preserves SLA commitments and auditability.
- ADR-04: Immutable audit logs with tenant-scoped partitioning
  - Selected option: All actions logged immutably per-tenant in a centralized Audit Log Service; supports tamper-evidence.
  - Rejected alternative: Decentralized, potentially mutable logs.
  - Rationale: Enables traceability, compliance, and forensic analysis.

10. Observability, testing, and rollout plan
- Observability
  - Metrics: p95 latency for redaction, classification, and routing; per-tenant queue depths; error rates; redaction confidence; rule version state; escalation counts.
  - Tracing: end-to-end traces across ingress, redaction, classification, routing, and human review.
  - Alerts: SLA breaches, high queue depth, redaction confidence failures, rule conflicts.
- Testing
  - Unit tests for each service; contract tests for inter-service messages.
  - Chaos engineering: inject latency, redaction failure, and rule conflicts in staging; verify fail-safe behavior.
  - Load testing: simulate 10k tickets/day with spikes; validate p95 <= 2s under normal load; measure tail latency under chaos.
- Rollout plan
  - Canary deployment for new redaction/classifier routing behavior per tenant subset.
  - Feature flags per tenant to enable/disable auto-routing; gradual rollout with rollback option.
  - Backward compatibility: ensure default routing uses safe, conservative paths during rollout.

11. Major risks and mitigations
- Risk: Redaction failures leaking PII
  - Mitigation: require redaction before ML calls; fallback to manual review; audit events.
- Risk: Latency tail spikes leading to SLA misses
  - Mitigation: autoscaling, backpressure, exponential backoff, escalation to human review when necessary.
- Risk: Conflicting tenant routing rules leading to inconsistent routing
  - Mitigation: versioned rules, conflict detection, admin resolution workflow.
- Risk: Data leakage across tenants due to shared components
  - Mitigation: strict tenant isolation in queues and storage; authorization checks; tenant-scoped data partitioning.
- Risk: Human review bottlenecks
  - Mitigation: optimized triage rules; improved automation with confidence scoring; SLAs for review; parallel review queues.

12. Final recommendation
- Adopt a modular, tenant-isolated architecture with a strong emphasis on privacy-first redaction prior to any external model usage.
- Implement per-tenant, versioned routing rules with conflict detection and admin conflict resolution workflows to prevent auto-routing ambiguities.
- Enforce fail-safe, non-dropping behavior by escalating to human review when latency or redaction confidence degrade, and by maintaining durable queues and audit trails.
- Invest in robust observability and chaos testing to validate p95 targets and resilience under deterministic failure injections A, B, and C.
- Use ADR-guided decisions to align engineering choices with privacy, reliability, and tenant-specific customization goals, while keeping costs and vendor references out of scope as requested.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Assumptions and non-goals
- Assumptions
  - Ingest rate: ~10,000 tickets/day (~7 per second on average), with bursts; 20 tenants with tenant-specific routing rules.
  - Tickets arrive in near-real-time and must be classified for issue type and urgency, with personal data redacted before any third-party model use.
  - All data processing must be tenant-isolated for auditability; PII handling complies with privacy expectations (no legal claims asserted).
  - A 2-second p95 latency target for automated classification and routing under normal load.
  - Fail-safety: no tickets dropped; if downstream components fail, tickets are queued and retried with safe fallback behavior.
- Non-goals
  - Real-time human triage beyond optional escalation.
  - Absolute zero latency; focus is p95 within target under normal load.
  - Vendor-specific implementations, exact cost figures, or legally required compliance claims not provided.

2. Component diagram described in text
- Ingest Layer
  - Ticket Ingestion API: receives tickets from customers, normalizes format, ensures idempotency.
  - Queueing System: decouples ingestion from processing; supports backpressure and retry semantics.

- Processing Core (stateless microservices per request)
  - Redaction Service: applies PII/PHI redaction rules, outputs redacted payload; emits confidence score.
  - Classification Service: issue-type classifier and urgency predictor; runs on redacted content; returns labels and confidence.
  - Routing Rules Engine: tenant-aware rules to map tickets to queues/teams; resolves conflicts with versioned rule sets.
  - Risk & Review Controller: determines if ticket is high-risk or uncertain; routes to human review queue when threshold met or confidence too low.
  - Security/Policy Guard: enforces policy checks, ensures data minimization, and enforces 3rd-party model call redaction gating.

- Data & Audit
  - Tenant Data Store: stores tenant metadata, routing rules, and audit trails; enforces tenant isolation.
  - Audit Logger: writes immutable, timestamped events per ticket; supports per-tenant access control.
  - PII/Privacy Vault: stores redaction and masking artifacts, access controlled and auditable.

- Routing & Delivery
  - Message Router: directs tickets to automated queues or human review queues; respects p95 latency budgets.
  - Human Review Queue: UI or API for analysts; ticket state updates propagate back to system.
  - Telemetry & Observability: metrics, traces, logs collection.

- Reliability & Resilience
  - Backpressure and Queuing: durable queues with dead-letter handling.
  - Retry & Circuit Breakers: bounded retries, circuit breakers around downstream components.
  - Fail-safe Gateways: fallback paths if components are unavailable; ensure tickets are preserved.

3. End-to-end data flow
- Ticket arrives at Ingest API → assigned a unique ticket_id → persisted briefly in Ingest Store → placed in Processing Queue.
- Redaction Service processes ticket: outputs redacted_payload + redaction_confidence; if redaction confidence is low but acceptable, proceed; if fail, escalate to human review.
- Classification Service consumes redacted_payload → outputs issue_type, urgency, and class_confidence.
- Routing Rules Engine uses tenant context and active routing rule version to map to: automated queue(s) (for fast resolution) or human review queue(s); if high-risk or low confidence, route to Human Review Controller.
- Risk & Review Controller flags high-risk or uncertain tickets; tickets are moved to Human Review Queue with required metadata for analysts.
- All state changes, decisions, and data access are logged to Audit Logger, including tenant_id, rule_version, redaction_confidence, class_confidence, routing_decision, and any retries.
- Final disposition is attached to ticket and surfaced to customer-support dashboards; automation continues processing other tickets; TPIs (third-party integrations) are invoked only on redacted payloads.

4. Key services and responsibilities table
- Ingestion Service
  - Responsibilities: receive, normalize, deduplicate, enqueue; idempotent entry.
- Redaction Service
  - Responsibilities: redact PII/PHI; produce redacted payload and confidence; deterministic output for given input.
- Classification Service
  - Responsibilities: issue-type classification, urgency estimation; provide confidence.
- Routing Rules Engine
  - Responsibilities: apply tenant-specific routing rules; manage rule-version & conflict resolution; provide routing decision.
- Risk & Review Controller
  - Responsibilities: evaluate high-risk or uncertain tickets; direct to human review when needed.
- Human Review Queue
  - Responsibilities: provide analyst-facing interface; track human decisions; feed back outcomes to system.
- Data & Privacy Vault
  - Responsibilities: securely store redaction metadata, PII masking artifacts, and audit data; enforce tenant isolation.
- Audit Logger
  - Responsibilities: immutable, time-ordered logs; tenant-scoped access.
- Telemetry & Observability
  - Responsibilities: collect metrics, traces, logs; support SLO verification and incident response.

5. Tenant isolation and privacy safeguards
- Data separation
  - Tenant-scoped data partitions; encryption at rest and in transit; access strictly per-tenant.
- Redaction-before-third-party
  - All data sent to any external model is redacted and sanitized; redaction_confidence gating determines if external calls are allowed or escalated.
- Least privilege
  - Microservices run with scoped service accounts; API keys rotated; no cross-tenant data leakage.
- Auditability
  - All actions logged with tenant_id, user_id (if applicable), and operation; immutable logs retained for regulatory windows.
- Policy enforcement
  - Privacy guard enforces PII handling rules; if PII is detected after redaction, escalate for review.

6. Reliability and failure-handling plan
- Durable queues with backpressure
  - Use persistent queues; if downstream slows, backpressure propagates upstream without dropping tickets.
- Safe retry semantics
  - Exponential backoff with jitter; max retry window; failed items moved to dead-letter if unrecoverable.
- Fail-safe routing
  - If routing engine or rules unavailable, default to conservative routing: escalate to human review or place in automated pending queue with strict visibility controls.
- Idempotence and ordering
  - Idempotent processing per ticket; maintain per-ticket sequencing to avoid duplicate actions.
- Observability-driven incident handling
  - Alerts on p95 latency breach, redaction confidence dips, or rule-version conflicts; auto-rollout rollback if critical.

7. Latency and throughput reasoning
- Target: p95 classification+routing within 2 seconds under normal load.
- Design choices to meet target
  - Parallelizable microservices: redaction, classification, and routing operate in parallel on independent threads where possible.
  - Batching strategy: micro-batching only for non-dependent components; avoid batching in redaction/classification to preserve latency.
  - Caching of tenant routing profiles and rule versions to reduce lookups.
  - Durable, fast queues (e.g., in-memory with persistence or optimized persistent queue) to minimize enqueue-dequeue latency.
  - Asynchronous human-review pathway ensures that automated path remains within 2 seconds; high-risk paths incur added latency but are designed for safe escalation.
- Worst-case considerations
  - Bursts: queue scaling with autoscaling of compute; p95 target maintained by buffering and fast path for low-risk tickets.
  - Redaction confidence dips: if redaction is uncertain, decision may stall automated path; fallback to human review to preserve latency and safety.

8. Failure-injection matrix for A, B, and C
A. The redaction service returns low confidence for a ticket containing possible PII.
- Observed state transition: Ticket enters redaction service → redaction confidence low; system detects potential PII not confidently redacted; triggers safety gate.
- User/operator visibility: Operations sees redaction confidence warning; analyst may see redaction anomaly; audit event notes redaction_low_confidence.
- Retry/recovery: Redaction service may retry once; if persistent, escalation to human review with redaction trace attached; no data leaked beyond redacted payload; if non-redactable, switch to human review path.
- Audit event: redaction_confidence_low, redaction_attempt, escalation_to_human_review.
- Final safe outcome: Ticket routed to Human Review Queue; automated path paused for this item; PII protection maintained; no data leakage.

B. The classifier reaches a 1.2-second timeout while queue depth is rising.
- Observed state transition: Classification service times out; downstream queue depth increases; latency budget threatened.
- User/operator visibility: Real-time latency dashboards show rising p95; alert triggered; analysts see item in pending state; audit shows timeout event.
- Retry/recovery: Retry with backoff; if still failing, route to Human Review for triage; potential scalar backoff of non-critical items.
- Audit event: classifier_timeout, queue_depth_rising, retry_attempt, escalation_decision.
- Final safe outcome: If automated path cannot complete within target, item escalated to human review; data remains intact; system maintains no drop.

C. Two administrators publish conflicting routing-rule versions for the same tenant.
- Observed state transition: Active rule version ambiguous; routing engine selects one; conflict detected.
- User/operator visibility: Admin console shows version conflict flag; operational notes indicate conflict; audit records show conflicting versions.
- Retry/recovery: Conflict resolution policy applies; system may enforce a merge or require admin merge; changes revert to last stable version or use a transactional lock until resolved.
- Audit event: routing_rule_conflict_detected, active_version, attempted_resolution, conflict_resolution_action.
- Final safe outcome: System uses a well-defined conflict resolution process (e.g., last-writer-wins with audit trail or enforced admin-approval); no tickets dropped; once resolved, routing proceeds with a known version.

9. Architecture decision records
- ADR 1: Redaction-before-third-party model calls
  - Selected option: Enforce redaction service before any third-party model invocation; send only redacted payload to external models.
  - Rejected alternative: Do not redact before external calls; send raw data to models and redact post-processing.
  - Rationale: Protects PII, minimizes risk of data leakage, aligns with privacy safeguards; simplifies governance of data sent to external systems; introduces deterministic gating on redaction confidence.

- ADR 2: Tenant-aware routing with versioned rule sets
  - Selected option: Maintain per-tenant routing rule sets with explicit versioning; rules are loaded into a fast in-memory cache with strict consistency; conflicts trigger admin-resolution workflow.
  - Rejected alternative: Single global routing policy; no per-tenant isolation.
  - Rationale: Enables tenant-specific routing, auditability, and compliance; avoids cross-tenant leakage; allows independent versioning and rollouts per tenant.

- ADR 3: Fail-safe escalation for high-risk/uncertain tickets
  - Selected option: Default to automated path for highest throughput, but escalate high-risk or uncertain tickets to Human Review; use a separate queue with deterministic routing.
  - Rejected alternative: Fail closed (drop or block) or fail open (send raw data to external models without escalation).
  - Rationale: Ensures no tickets are dropped; preserves safety by ensuring potential issues are reviewed by humans; maintains SLA focus on low-latency automated path for typical tickets.

4. Observability, testing, and rollout plan
- Observability
  - Metrics: p95 latency for classification+routing, per-tenant throughput, redaction_confidence distribution, classifier_confidence, queue depths, error rates.
  - Tracing: end-to-end traces across Ingest → Redaction → Classification → Routing → Human Review; correlate with tenant_id and rule_version.
  - Logging: immutable audit trails with per-ticket events; access controls for tenants to view audit logs.
  - Dashboards: show SLO adherence, failure-injection status, and fault domains.

- Testing
  - Unit tests: redaction correctness, classifier accuracy, routing rule resolution.
  - Integration tests: end-to-end flows with mock third-party models, redaction gating, and human-review paths.
  - Chaos engineering: controlled failure injections A, B, and C to validate safety nets; ensure no data loss.
  - Privacy tests: verify PII is never transmitted to external models; validate redaction coverage.

- Rollout plan
  - Phase 1: Deploy in isolated staging with synthetic data; run A/B experiments on routing decisions; monitor latency.
  - Phase 2: Canary rollout to subset of tenants; observe SLA metrics and conflict handling.
  - Phase 3: Gradual broad rollout with auto-rollout disabled by default until confidence established; implement feature flags for routing rule versions.
  - Phase 4: Full production with ongoing review and automatic rollback on SLO breach or safety stop.

5. Major risks and mitigations
- Risk: Redaction confidence drops systematically
  - Mitigation: Improve redaction model with additional training data; bypass external calls for low-confidence cases; escalate to human review with explicit caveats.
- Risk: Classifier timeout under load
  - Mitigation: Scale classification service horizontally; implement fast-path for high-confidence cases; prefer asynchronous processing for slower items; implement queue depth alerts.
- Risk: Conflicting routing rule versions
  - Mitigation: Enforce strict versioned rule deployment; require admin approvals for conflicts; implement last-writer-wins with audit resolution path; lock rule sets during active processing.
- Risk: Data leakage through misconfigured components
  - Mitigation: Enforce strict data access policies; audit all data flows; automated checks for PII in outbound payloads.
- Risk: Failure to meet p95 latency under burst
  - Mitigation: Implement autoscaling, pre-wetched routing decisions, and precomputed tenant routing profiles; degrade gracefully to human review for extremely high load scenarios without dropping tickets.

6. Final recommendation
- Adopt a tenant-aware, privacy-first, fail-safe multi-tenant AI triage architecture as outlined. Prioritize redaction-before-model calls, per-tenant routing rule versioning, and escalation to human review for high-risk or uncertain tickets. Ensure durability and no data drops via durable queues and robust retry strategies, with observable latency budgets and chaos-tested resilience. Implement ADR-aligned decisions (redaction gating, per-tenant rules, and escalation policy) and maintain robust auditing to satisfy tenant and operator needs while preserving safety and privacy.

7. Notable design choices summary
- Redaction-first for all 3rd-party calls to minimize data exposure.
- Tenant-specific routing rules with versioning and conflict-resolution workflow.
- Automated classification with p95 target under normal load, plus safe escalation for high-risk/uncertain cases.
- No ticket drops; robust backpressure, retries, and durable queues.
- Comprehensive audit logs and tenant isolation throughout.

8. Appendices (quick references)
- A. Failure-injection matrix summaries (A, B, C) described in section 8 above.
- B. ADRs: Redaction-before-model calls; Tenant-aware routing; Fail-safe escalation. 
- C. Observability plan: metrics, tracing, logging, dashboards.

If you’d like, I can convert this into a diagrammatic representation or provide concrete pseudocode for the processing pipeline and failure handlers.

2. Component diagram described in text
- Ingestion Layer
  - API gateway with per-tenant context, rate limiting, basic validation
  - Durable input queues with per-tenant routing keys
- Privacy Guard
  - Redaction Service: PII/PHI detection and redaction with a redaction confidence output

- Classification Layer

  - Metrics, traces, dashboards, alerting
- Storage Layer
  - Durable storage for tickets, classifications, routing decisions, and audit trails
- Failure Handling / Safe Fall-Back
  - Dead-letter queues, backpressure signaling, graceful degradation paths

3. End-to-end data flow
- Ticket arrives with tenant context; validated and persisted; enqueued per-tenant.
- Redaction Service processes raw ticket; outputs redacted payload and redaction confidence.
- Redaction Gate ensures only redacted content proceeds to model calls.
- Classification Layer runs issue-type and urgency classifiers on redacted content; emits results and latency metrics.
- Routing Engine applies tenant routing rules; decides automated path vs human review.
- If automated: persist results, deliver to downstream handlers, and write tenant-audit events.
- If high-risk/uncertain: place ticket in Human Review Queue; log routing decision and awaiting disposition.
- All actions are tenant-scoped in audit logs; observability collects end-to-end traces.
- On failure: messages are retried with backoff; if unrecoverable, move to dead-letter with safe fallback; no ticket is dropped.

4. Key services and responsibilities table
- Ingestion Service
  - Receive tickets, basic validation, attach tenant context, enqueue
- Redaction Service
  - Detect redaction needs, redact content, emit redaction confidence, provide redacted payload
- Classifier Service
  - Issue-type and urgency classification with confidence; latency tracking
- Policy/Routes Store (Tenant Config)
  - Tenant-specific routing rules, per-versioned policy data
- Routing Engine
  - Evaluate rules, resolve conflicts, decide automated vs human routing
- Human Review Queue/UI
  - Present high-risk tickets; capture disposition; provide feedback
- Audit Log Service
  - Append-only, tenant-scoped audit trails for all actions
- Data Security Layer
  - Access control, encryption, data minimization enforcement
- Observability Layer
  - Metrics, traces, dashboards, alerts
- Storage Layer
  - Durable ticket and audit data stores
- Failure Handling Layer
  - Dead-letter queues, backpressure, circuit breakers, safe-fall paths

5. Tenant isolation and privacy safeguards
- Data segregation: per-tenant namespaces for queues, stores, and audit logs; strict RBAC
- Redaction-first policy: redaction occurs before any third-party calls; raw data never leaves boundary unredacted
- Per-tenant routing rules: versioned policy data; rules evaluated against active version; conflict detection
- Immutable audit logs: tenant-scoped, tamper-evident logs with versioned references
- PII minimization: only necessary fields retained; redacted data used downstream
- Access controls: least-privilege service identities; mTLS between services; tenant-aware UI views

6. Reliability and failure-handling plan
- No-dropped-tickets guarantee: durable queues, backpressure, idempotent processing, and dead-letter handling
- Safe fallback: degraded path routes tickets to human review when components degrade or latency targets are threatened
- Backpressure and autoscaling: queue-depth-based scaling with per-tenant isolation to prevent cross-tenant interference
- Retry semantics: bounded exponential backoff with jitter; retries logged in audit
- Safe state transitions: clear pending/automated/human-review states with auditable transitions
- Audit-first discipline: all state changes and failures captured in tenant-scoped audit logs

7. Latency and throughput reasoning
- Target: p95 ≤ 2 seconds for automated classification and routing under normal load
- Critical path: Ingestion → Redaction → Classification → Routing → Persistence
- Parallelism: potential overlap between redaction and classification on safe inputs; per-tenant sharding reduces cross-tenant contention
- Queueing: fast, durable queues with backpressure; per-tenant sinks to prevent hot spots
- Caching: cache active routing rule versions per tenant; lightweight lookups for policy engine
- Backstops: if latency exceeds targets, unblocked path is human review; safeguard ensures no data loss

8. Failure-injection matrix for A, B, and C
A. Redaction service returns low confidence for possible PII
- Observed state: redaction confidence flagged as low; high-PII risk triggers escalation path
- Visibility: audit logs show redaction_confidence and escalation status; operators see redaction anomaly flags
- Retry/recovery: retry with intensified redaction or route to human review; do not send unredacted data downstream
- Audit event: redaction_confidence_low, escalation_to_human_review
- Final safe outcome: either reprocess with stronger redaction or handoff to human review; no leakage occurs

B. Classifier timeout (1.2s) while queue depth rising
- Observed state: classifier timeout; latency breach; backlog grows
- Visibility: latency dashboards alert; tickets marked pending; operators may intervene
- Retry/recovery: exponential backoff; if persistent, escalate to human review or scale classifier workers
- Audit event: classifier_timeout, queue_depth_warning, retry_attempt
- Final safe outcome: automated path degraded but preserved; escalated to human review to maintain SLA and safety

C. Two admins publish conflicting routing-rule versions for same tenant
- Observed state: active policy ambiguous; routing engine detects conflict
- Visibility: admin UI shows conflict; audit logs record both versions and resolution steps
- Retry/recovery: conflict-resolution workflow to reconcile; lock policy during resolution; eventual consistency via versioning policy
- Audit event: routing_rule_conflict_detected, versions_in_conflict, resolution_status
- Final safe outcome: single active version chosen after resolution; no misrouting; automated routing resumes with clear audit trail

9. Architecture decision records
- ADR-01: Redaction-before-third-party model calls
  - Selected: Enforce redaction gate before any external model invocation; send only redacted payload
  - Rejected: Redaction after model call or optional redaction per route
  - Rationale: Prevents PII leakage to external models; standardizes data-sanitized inputs
- ADR-02: Per-tenant routing rule versioning with conflict detection
  - Selected: Each tenant has versioned rule sets; conflicts block auto-routing until resolved
  - Rejected: Global or non-versioned routing; immediate effect of changes
  - Rationale: Enables tenant-specific customization, auditability, and safe rollout/rollback
- ADR-03: Fail-safe escalation for latency/health degradation
  - Selected: Escalate high-risk/latency-breaches to human review; avoid silent retries beyond safety margins
  - Rejected: Continuous automated retries without escalation
  - Rationale: Safeguards against misrouting and data leakage under instability
- ADR-04: Immutable, tenant-scoped audit logs
  - Selected: Append-only, tenant-scoped logs with tamper-evident properties
  - Rejected: Centralized mutable logs without tenant partitioning
  - Rationale: Strong traceability, isolation, and forensic capability
- ADR-05: Backpressure-driven autoscaling plan
  - Selected: Tie workers to queue depth; per-tenant and global autoscaling with cooldowns
  - Rejected: Fixed capacity or reactive-only scaling
  - Rationale: Maintains p95 targets under load while avoiding thrash

10. Observability, testing, and rollout plan
- Observability
  - Metrics: p95 latency for redaction, classification, and routing; per-tenant queue depths; redaction confidence distribution; rule-version state; escalation counts
  - Tracing: end-to-end traces across ingestion, redaction, classification, routing, human review
  - Logging: tenant-scoped audit logs with versioned policy references
  - Dashboards: SLA adherence, failure injections, and fault-domain visibility
- Testing
  - Unit tests: redaction correctness, classifier outputs, policy resolution
  - Integration tests: end-to-end flows with mock third-party models; human-review paths
  - Chaos testing: deterministic injections A, B, C to validate fail-safes
  - Load testing: simulate 10k/day with bursts; verify p95 ≤ 2s under normal load
- Rollout plan
  - Phase 1: staging with synthetic data; validate redaction gating and rule-version resolution
  - Phase 2: canary per-tenant rollout; monitor SLA metrics and conflict handling
  - Phase 3: gradual production rollout with feature flags for routing rules and redaction behavior
  - Phase 4: full production with rollback capability on safety-slash-SLA breaches

11. Major risks and mitigations
- PII leakage if redaction fails
  - Mitigation: redaction-first gating, multi-pass checks, escalation to human review for low confidence
- Latency tail spikes
  - Mitigation: autoscale, per-tenant sharding, backpressure, and safe human-review escalation
- Policy conflicts across tenants
  - Mitigation: versioned routing rules, conflict detection, admin-resolution workflow
- Cross-tenant data leakage
  - Mitigation: strict tenant isolation, per-tenant namespaces, RBAC, and auditability
- Human-review bottlenecks
  - Mitigation: SLA targets for reviews, improved automation confidence scoring, parallel queues

12. Final recommendation
- Proceed with a tenant-aware, privacy-first multi-tenant AI triage architecture that enforces redaction-before-model calls, per-tenant versioned routing rules with conflict resolution, and fail-safe escalation to human review for high-risk or degraded conditions. Ensure durability with backpressure-driven queues and immutable, tenant-scoped audit logs. Emphasize robust observability and deterministic failure tests (A, B, C) to validate resilience and SLA adherence. Use ADR-guided decisions to maintain privacy, reliability, and tenant-specific customization, while avoiding vendor or cost specifics.

- ADR-03: Fail-safe escalation for latency/degradation
  - Selected: Default to human review under degraded performance; avoid unsafe auto-action.
  - Rejected alternative: Continue auto-routing with degraded defaults or drop.
  - Rationale: Prioritizes safety, privacy, and SLA integrity; prevents silent failures.
- ADR-04: Immutable, tenant-scoped audit logs
  - Selected: Append-only, tenant-scoped audit logs; tamper-evident storage.
  - Rejected alternative: Centralized, mutable logs.
  - Rationale: Enables per-tenant traceability, compliance, forensics.
- ADR-05: Backpressure-driven autoscaling
  - Selected: Scale workers based on queue depth; per-tenant and global controls; avoid thrashing.
  - Rejected alternative: Fixed capacity; reactive scaling only after outages.
  - Rationale: Maintains p95 targets during bursts; controlled scalability to prevent instability.

10. Observability, testing, and rollout plan
- Observability
  - Metrics: p95 latency for redaction, classification, and routing; per-tenant queue depths; redaction_confidence distribution; rule_version state; escalation counts.
  - Tracing: end-to-end traces across ingress → redaction → classification → routing → human review.
  - Logging: tenant-scoped audit logs with rule_version references; immutable storage.
  - Dashboards: SLA adherence, fault domains, failure-injection readiness.
- Testing
  - Unit tests: redaction correctness, classifier accuracy, policy resolution.
  - Integration tests: end-to-end with mock third-party calls; verify redaction gating; human-review path.
  - Chaos testing: deterministic failure injections A, B, C to validate fail-safes.
  - Load testing: simulate 10k/day with bursts; ensure p95 target and no data loss.
- Rollout plan
  - Canary: limited tenants; monitor latency and correctness.
  - Gradual rollout: feature flags for routing rules and redaction behavior; controlled exposure.
  - Rollback: quick rollback path for policy or redaction logic; maintain previous stable versions.
  - Incident playbooks: standard procedures for A, B, C with escalation steps.

11. Major risks and mitigations
- PII leakage risk if redaction fails
  - Mitigation: redaction gate mandatory; multi-pass checks; escalate to human review for low-confidence cases; immutable audit logs.
- Latency tail risk
  - Mitigation: autoscale; per-tenant sharding; backpressure; fast-path for high-confidence cases; escalate when needed.
- Policy conflict and inconsistent routing
  - Mitigation: strict versioning; conflict detection; admin resolution workflow; tenant isolation maintained.
- Data leakage across tenants
  - Mitigation: strict partitioning of data stores and queues; per-tenant access controls; auditability.
- Human-review bottlenecks
  - Mitigation: SLA targets for reviews; prioritization; scalable review queues and improved automation with confidence scoring.

12. Final recommendation
- Proceed with a tenant-aware, redaction-first, multi-tenant AI triage architecture as outlined.
- Commit to ADRs: redaction-before-model calls; per-tenant versioned routing with conflict detection; fail-safe escalation to human review under degradation.
- Ensure durable, tenant-scoped audit logs and robust observability.
- Validate resilience via deterministic failure injections A, B, and C and iterate on tuning autoscaling, backpressure, and admin workflows.
- Emphasize staged rollout, canaries, and robust rollback procedures to preserve SLA targets and data integrity.

If you’d like, I can provide a diagrammatic mapping or pseudocode for the processing pipeline, including redaction gate, classifier call, routing decision, and failure handlers.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Critique competing proposals and prior critiques. Verify numerical facts, sources, hard constraints, and required output elements; reject an attractive recommendation when its premises are unsupported.
intermediate_messages.content:
Critical analysis of the three proposals (Proposal 1, 2, 3) against the task requirements. I verify constraints, identify gaps, and recommend concrete refinements. I avoid vendor, cost, or unprovided performance data.

1) Assumptions and non-goals
- All three proposals claim ~10k tickets/day, 20 tenants, 2s p95 for automated classification/routing, redaction before third-party calls, fail-safe (no drop). These align with the prompt, but the documents do not consistently quantify burst behavior, per-tenant distribution, or precise p95 under peak load. Recommendation: explicitly define peak throughput, tail latency targets (e.g., p99), and burst-ability per tenant; specify acceptable backlog queue depths and backpressure thresholds.

2) Component diagrams and end-to-end data flow
- All proposals describe a redaction gate preceding model calls, per-tenant routing rules with versioning, human-review paths, and tenant-aware audit logs. They are generally consistent with the base requirements.
- Gaps to address in all:
  - Exact data isolation guarantees across tenants in queues, storage, and audit stores (e.g., namespace isolation, RBAC mappings, mTLS between services). Proposals mention segmentation but lack concrete isolation mechanisms.
  - Immutable audit log design specifics (append-only store, cryptographic signing, retention windows). Vague on tamper-evidence guarantees and cross-tenant query capabilities.
  - Failure-handling details for “fail safely without dropping tickets” are high-level. Need concrete dead-letter, retry/backoff caps, and ordering guarantees to guarantee no data loss.
  - Observability and tracing detail: cross-service traces, tenant-scoped correlation IDs, and per-tenant SLO dashboards are mentioned but not standardized. Require a unified trace context and standardized metrics definitions.

3) End-to-end data flow specifics
- Pros:
  - Redaction-first policy is consistently enforced in all proposals.
  - Tenant-specific routing with versioning is present, enabling safe rollouts and rollback.
- Cons:
  - Some proposals imply dual-path routing and late binding across tenants; ensure consistency in how redaction confidence interacts with routing decisions (avoid non-deterministic routing due to confidence variance).
  - Human Review allocation is important; proposals should quantify expected human throughput and SLAs to avoid bottlenecks. No numeric SLA targets for human throughput are provided.

4) Key services and responsibilities
- Proposals provide comprehensive service lists but diverge in some names (e.g., ADRs vs. routing engine). This is acceptable as long as responsibilities map cleanly.
- Critical missing detail in all:
  - Concrete data models for tickets, redaction output, classifier results, and routing decisions (schemas, version references, IDs).
  - Idempotency guarantees across retries and the exact effect of reprocessing on audit logs and state machines.

5) Tenant isolation and privacy safeguards
- Strengths:
  - All emphasize per-tenant partitions, redaction-before-model calls, and immutable audit logs.
- Gaps:
  - No explicit multi-tenant encryption strategy for data at rest vs. in transit beyond generic statements.
  - Access control model details (RBAC for services, service-to-service authentication, key management) are underspecified.
  - PII handling policy enforcement needs concrete controls (data minimization rules, field-level redaction templates, revocation/rotation policies).

6) Reliability and failure-handling plan
- Strengths:
  - Durable queues, backpressure, dead-letter queues, and fail-safe routing to human review are common.
- Gaps:
  - Clear, deterministic failover sequencing across components (which component must recover first, dependencies, and ordering).
  - Explicit idempotency guarantees and exactly-once processing semantics where required.
  - Defined timeouts and circuit-breaker policies for each downstream dependency (model calls, redaction, routing service, human queue systems).
  - Recovery playbooks and runbooks for deterministic failure scenarios A, B, C (they are described in 8, but need integrated operator runbooks).

7) Latency and throughput reasoning
- Proposals attempt to justify 2s p95 with parallelism and backpressure.
- Gaps:
  - No per-stage latency budgets, queue-depth thresholds, or autoscaling trigger formulas.
  - No discussion of caching for routing rules or model results, which could materially affect p95.
  - Need a formal queueing model (M/M/1 or M/G/1 with multiple servers) to estimate p95 under bursts; without this, the 2s target is speculative.

8) Failure-injection matrix for A, B, C (A, B, C present in all; some variance)
- Strengths:
  - Proposals A, B, C are incorporated with observed state transitions, visibility, retry/recovery, audits, and final safe outcomes.
- Gaps:
  - The matrices are descriptive but not consistently aligned to a single canonical state machine. It would help to formalize: state definitions, transition probabilities, and concrete operator actions for each injection.

9) Architecture decision records (ADRs)
- ADRs proposed include redaction-before-model calls, per-tenant versioned routing rules with conflict detection, and fail-safe escalation.
- Strengths:
  - ADRs cover privacy, tenant customization, and safety.
- Gaps:
  - ADRs should include rollback semantics, governance workflow, and conflict-resolution policies (e.g., how to merge conflicting rule versions, who approves merges, how to lock versions during processing).
  - ADRs should explicitly define data-retention, audit-log integrity guarantees, and compliance boundaries.

10) Observability, testing, and rollout plan
- Strengths:
  - Emphasize end-to-end tracing, per-tenant metrics, chaos testing, and staged rollouts.
- Gaps:
  - No explicit SRE-like service-level objectives (SLOs) for each service besides the 2s target; require concrete SLOs and error budgets per component.
  - Rollout plan lacks a clear rollback/kill-switch criteria tied to SLO breaches or privacy incidents.
  - Testing plan should include deterministic failure-injection scripts for A, B, C with expected recovery times and dashboards to verify.

11) Major risks and mitigations
- Covered risks align with privacy, latency, policy conflicts, data leakage, and human-review bottlenecks.
- Gaps:
  - Risk of inconsistent state when concurrent rule-version updates occur; need a distributed consensus or locking mechanism.
  - Security risk: admin interfaces for per-tenant routing rules could be misconfigured; require pre-production policy validation and change control.

12) Final recommendation
- All proposals present a solid baseline architecture with redaction-first and tenant-aware routing. However:
  - None provide enough concrete, enforceable metrics, data models, and deterministic state machines necessary to guarantee 2s p95 and no drops under bursts.
  - They lack explicit, testable Contingency Playbooks for deterministic injections A, B, C with machine- and human-mediated recovery steps.
- Recommended improvements to any proposal you pursue:
  - Formalize a single, canonical data model and state machine for Ticket -> RedactedPayload -> Classified -> Routed -> Archived, with explicit versioned routing rules and a per-tenant policy registry.
  - Define explicit latency budgets per stage, queue depth thresholds, autoscaling triggers, and a formal capacity plan including per-tenant sharding strategy.
  - Implement strong tenant isolation: per-tenant queues, per-tenant storage namespaces, and cryptographic audit-log integrity with tenant-scoped access.
  - Define exact retry/backoff limits, idempotent processing guarantees, and dead-letter handling across all services.
  - Strengthen ADRs with: rollback procedures, conflict-resolution workflows, governance for rule versioning, and a safety-first escalation policy with measurable SLAs for human-review throughput.
  - Create a rigorous failure-injection plan with deterministic acceptance criteria, including pre/post-conditions, operator-visible indicators, and automated remediation scripts.

Final verdict
- The proposals are largely aligned with the requested architecture but are insufficiently concrete to guarantee the 2s p95 target, deterministic handling of A/B/C injections, and guaranteed no-ticket-drop behavior under all bursts. Choose a single integrated design that:
  - Enforces redaction-before-model calls with explicit confidence gating and mandatory escalation for low-confidence redaction.
  - Uses per-tenant, versioned routing rules with a robust conflict-detection and admin-resolution workflow.
  - Implements fail-safe default routing to human review under degradation, plus durable queues with strict backoff and dead-letter handling.
  - Defines end-to-end data models, explicit latency budgets, capacity planning, and strict tenant isolation.
  - Incorporates comprehensive observability and formal failure-injection testing plans to verify resilience before production rollout.
